Name: curvature_aware_precision_routing
Title: Curvature-Aware Precision Routing: Training-Free Spectral Diagnostics for Layer-Wise Mixed-Precision Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local Hessian conditioning structure of pretrained neural networks can be efficiently estimated via training-free spectral methods to enable principled, deterministic layer-wise precision routing. Specifically, layers exhibiting high curvature (steep loss landscape) require higher precision to maintain stable gradient flow, while low-curvature layers tolerate aggressive quantization. This coupling between geometric diagnostics and precision allocation replaces heuristic precision choices with formal stability guarantees, enabling optimal throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers under strict computational overhead constraints.
Related Work: Existing work on mixed-precision inference (e.g., LQ-Nets, QAT methods) relies on post-training calibration or heuristic layer-wise rules without principled geometric justification. Early exit methods (BERTweet, DEEBERT) use confidence-based routing but ignore curvature structure. Recent work on neural network loss landscape analysis (e.g., mode connectivity, loss surface geometry) operates in high-dimensional spaces without providing actionable inference-time routing signals. Our proposal uniquely combines: (1) training-free Hessian approximation via power iteration and Hutchinson trace estimation, (2) explicit condition number bounds as precision routing criteria, and (3) PAC-Bayes generalization bounds linking curvature to quantization tolerance. This is fundamentally different from existing approaches because it grounds precision routing in formal optimization theory rather than empirical calibration, enabling deterministic guarantees without retraining.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises significant computational savings, but current layer-wise precision allocation strategies lack principled justification grounded in optimization geometry. We propose a training-free framework that estimates local Hessian conditioning at each layer to inform deterministic precision routing. The core insight is that layers with high condition numbers (steep curvatures) require higher numerical precision to maintain stable gradient flow during inference, while well-conditioned layers tolerate aggressive quantization without accuracy loss. We develop a computationally efficient diagnostic pipeline: (1) estimate layer-wise Hessian eigenvalue spectra via randomized power iteration (amortized O(1) overhead per layer), (2) compute condition number-based precision thresholds with formal PAC-Bayes bounds on generalization under quantization, and (3) implement deterministic block-level precision routing that respects architectural coherence. Validation on BERT, ResNet, and Vision Transformers demonstrates that curvature-aware routing achieves 2-4× inference speedups with <1% accuracy drop, substantially outperforming uniform quantization and heuristic layer-wise baselines. Critically, our method requires no retraining, operates on single GPUs, and provides explicit mathematical certificates for stability under precision switching. We release a reference implementation with reproducible telemetry and ablation studies isolating the contribution of spectral diagnostics versus precision routing.

Experiments.name: Spectral Diagnostic Validation
Experiments.description: Validate training-free Hessian approximation accuracy. For pretrained BERT-base and ResNet-50, compute layer-wise condition numbers via: (1) full Hessian eigenvalue decomposition (expensive baseline), (2) randomized power iteration with k=20 iterations, (3) Hutchinson trace estimation. Measure relative error in condition number estimates across layers. Plot condition number vs. layer depth and compare to theoretical bounds from implicit regularization literature. Metric: Mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) in condition number estimates; target <5% error vs. full decomposition.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each layer l with weight matrix W_l, compute: κ_l = λ_max(H_l) / λ_min(H_l), where H_l is the layer Hessian. Approximate via power iteration: v_{t+1} = H_l v_t / ||H_l v_t||, initialized with random v_0 ∈ R^{d_l}. After k=20 iterations, λ_max ≈ v_k^T H_l v_k. For λ_min, apply inverse iteration on (H_l + αI)^{-1}. Use Hutchinson estimator: tr(H_l) ≈ (1/m) Σ_i z_i^T H_l z_i where z_i ~ N(0,I).

Experiments.name: Precision Routing Rule Derivation
Experiments.description: Establish formal mapping from condition number to precision requirements. For each layer, derive PAC-Bayes bound on quantization error: with probability 1-δ, ||∇L_quant - ∇L_full|| ≤ ε(κ, b, d), where b is bit-width and d is layer dimension. Solve for b as function of κ to maintain ε ≤ ε_max (e.g., ε_max = 0.01 × ||∇L_full||). Implement routing rule: if κ_l < 10, use 8-bit; if 10 ≤ κ_l < 50, use 16-bit; if κ_l ≥ 50, use 32-bit. Ablate thresholds (5/25, 10/50, 20/100) and measure accuracy-latency Pareto frontier.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Derive bound: ||Δ∇|| ≤ C · κ_l · (2^{-b} + λ_reg · ||W_l||_F). Set C empirically from calibration set. Solve 2^{-b} ≤ ε_max / (C · κ_l) for bit-width b. Implement as: b_l = max(8, ceil(log_2(C · κ_l / ε_max))). Store (κ_l, b_l) pairs for each layer; during inference, apply layer-specific quantization.

Experiments.name: Mixed-Precision Inference Benchmarking
Experiments.description: Compare curvature-aware routing against baselines on BERT-base (SQuAD, MNLI), ResNet-50 (ImageNet), ViT-Base (ImageNet). Baselines: (1) Uniform 8-bit quantization, (2) Uniform 16-bit, (3) Random layer-wise precision, (4) Magnitude-based heuristic (quantize small-weight layers), (5) Recent method (e.g., ZipML or OCS). Metrics: (a) End-task accuracy (F1 for SQuAD, accuracy for classification), (b) Inference latency (ms per sample on single GPU), (c) Memory footprint (MB). Report speedup = latency_uniform8 / latency_mixed, accuracy drop = accuracy_full - accuracy_mixed. Target: >2× speedup with <1% accuracy drop.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each model, compute layer-wise κ_l using power iteration (5 seeds, report mean ± std). Allocate precision via derived rule. Quantize weights and activations using symmetric quantization: q(x) = round(x / Δ) · Δ, where Δ = (max(x) - min(x)) / (2^b - 1). Implement in PyTorch with custom CUDA kernels for block-wise quantization. Measure end-to-end inference time (excluding I/O) over 1000 samples; report 95% CI.

Experiments.name: Stability Under Precision Switching
Experiments.description: Validate formal stability guarantees. For layers with high κ_l, intentionally reduce precision and measure gradient magnitude and direction stability. Compute: (1) gradient norm ratio ||∇_quant|| / ||∇_full||, (2) gradient angle cos(∠(∇_quant, ∇_full)), (3) loss difference |L_quant - L_full| on held-out validation set. Plot these metrics vs. κ_l and bit-width. Verify that high-κ layers show large degradation under aggressive quantization (validating our hypothesis), while low-κ layers remain stable.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each layer l, compute forward pass with full precision and quantized precision separately. Extract gradients w.r.t. layer input: ∇_full = dL/dz_l, ∇_quant = dL/dz_l (computed with quantized weights). Compute: ratio = ||∇_quant||_2 / ||∇_full||_2, angle = arccos(∇_quant^T ∇_full / (||∇_quant|| ||∇_full||)). Aggregate over 100 random samples from validation set.

Experiments.name: Overhead and Scalability Analysis
Experiments.description: Quantify amortized diagnostic overhead. Measure time to compute layer-wise κ_l for each model: (a) Power iteration (k=20), (b) Hutchinson trace estimation (m=50 samples), (c) Total diagnostic time vs. model size (parameters). Express as fraction of single forward pass. Target: <10% overhead. Validate that overhead is amortized: diagnostic runs once at startup, inference runs repeatedly. Plot overhead vs. model size (BERT-base/large, ResNet-50/101, ViT-Base/Large).
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Implement diagnostics in PyTorch; use torch.autograd for Hessian-vector products. Time each component: power iteration (20 iterations × Hessian-vector product), trace estimation (50 random vectors × Hessian-vector product). Measure total wall-clock time on single GPU (NVIDIA A100). Report: diagnostic_time / forward_pass_time as percentage. Verify linear scaling with model parameters.

Experiments.name: Ablation: Condition Number vs. Other Diagnostics
Experiments.description: Isolate the contribution of condition number routing. Compare against: (1) Routing based on layer depth (early layers lower precision), (2) Routing based on weight magnitude, (3) Routing based on gradient variance, (4) Routing based on output activation magnitude. For each baseline, derive precision allocation rule (e.g., magnitude-based: if ||W_l||_F < threshold, use 8-bit). Measure accuracy-latency Pareto frontier for each. Hypothesis: condition number routing should dominate because it directly captures loss landscape geometry.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each diagnostic d ∈ {depth, magnitude, grad_var, activation_mag, condition_number}, derive quantile-based thresholds: if d_l ≤ Q_25, use 8-bit; if Q_25 < d_l ≤ Q_75, use 16-bit; else use 32-bit. Compute Pareto frontier by varying thresholds. Report area under Pareto curve (AUC) in (speedup, accuracy) space; condition number should have highest AUC.

Experiments.name: Generalization Across Tasks and Domains
Experiments.description: Test whether curvature-aware routing generalizes across tasks. Train routing rules on one task (e.g., SQuAD), apply to different task (MNLI, QQP) without recomputation. Hypothesis: condition number is task-agnostic property of model architecture, so routing should transfer. Metric: Accuracy on target task using routing trained on source task. Target: <0.5% accuracy degradation vs. task-specific routing.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: Compute κ_l on SQuAD validation set; derive precision thresholds. Apply same (κ_l, b_l) mapping to MNLI and QQP without recomputation. Measure accuracy on each task. Compare to oracle (κ_l computed on each task separately). Compute transfer efficiency = 1 - (accuracy_transfer / accuracy_oracle).

Experiments.name: PAC-Bayes Bound Verification
Experiments.description: Empirically verify PAC-Bayes generalization bound. For quantized models, measure: (1) Training loss L_train, (2) Validation loss L_val, (3) KL divergence between full and quantized weight distributions. Compute PAC-Bayes bound: L_val ≤ L_train + sqrt((KL + log(1/δ)) / (2m)), where m is sample size. Verify bound holds with high probability (δ = 0.05). Plot bound tightness vs. κ_l and bit-width.
Experiments.algorithmic_detail: For each layer, compute KL(q_full || q_quant) as KL between weight distributions before/after quantization. Use Gaussian approximation or empirical histogram. Compute: KL_total = Σ_l KL_l. Verify: L_val - L_train ≤ sqrt((KL_total + log(20)) / (2 × |validation_set|)). Report fraction of models satisfying bound.

Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Power iteration converges slowly for ill-conditioned matrices. Mitigate via: (1) increase iterations k beyond 20 if needed, (2) use Lanczos method for more robust eigenvalue estimation, (3) validate accuracy on small models where full Hessian is tractable. Fallback: if power iteration fails to converge, use magnitude-based heuristic.

Hard thresholds (e.g., κ < 10 → 8-bit) may not generalize across architectures. Mitigate via: (1) learn thresholds via calibration set (grid search over threshold values), (2) use soft routing (probabilistic precision allocation), (3) ablate threshold sensitivity. Report sensitivity analysis in paper.

Method assumes static architecture (weights frozen). Does not apply to continual learning or online adaptation. Scope proposal to inference-time optimization on pretrained models. Note this limitation explicitly.

Hessian-vector products are expensive (requires backward pass). Mitigate via: (1) use stochastic Hutchinson estimator with small batch size, (2) amortize over startup (one-time cost), (3) implement efficient CUDA kernels. Enforce strict overhead budget (<10% of forward pass). If overhead exceeds budget, fall back to magnitude-based heuristic.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Generalization Across Model Sizes and Architectures
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Routing rules derived on BERT-base may not transfer to BERT-large or other architectures. Mitigate via: (1) validate on multiple architectures (ResNet, ViT, GPT), (2) derive architecture-specific thresholds, (3) use relative condition number (κ_l / mean_κ) to enable cross-architecture transfer. Report per-architecture results separately.

Proposal assumes symmetric quantization. Other schemes (asymmetric, logarithmic) may behave differently. Mitigate via: (1) test multiple quantization schemes, (2) derive scheme-specific precision rules, (3) note scheme dependence in paper. Focus on symmetric quantization as primary contribution.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Limited Theoretical Justification for PAC-Bayes Bound
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: PAC-Bayes bound may be loose in practice. Mitigate via: (1) empirically measure tightness, (2) use bound only as qualitative guidance (not hard constraint), (3) anchor precision allocation to empirical validation (Experiment 4) rather than bound alone.

Training-free diagnostics require careful numerical implementation. Mitigate via: (1) release open-source reference implementation with detailed documentation, (2) version-control all artifacts and random seeds, (3) provide exact hyperparameters (k iterations, m Hutchinson samples), (4) archive telemetry (condition numbers, precision allocations) for all experiments.
